workflow_patterns.overview:
LangGraph workflow patterns for multi-agent systems. In LangGraph, "workflows" are graphs — StateGraph instances with nodes and edges. This file covers the common patterns: sequential pipeline, parallel fan-out, conditional routing, streaming, and long-running async tasks. All patterns build on StateGraph and MemorySaver from 2_orchestration_core.xml.
CRITICAL: Code in this file shows STRUCTURAL PATTERNS only — not current syntax. Always run MCP queries for the specific pattern before implementing. LangGraph's Send API, streaming API, and routing API change across releases.

url.label: LangGraph: parallel execution with Send API (map-reduce)

docs_reference.instruction: Before generating workflow code, query MCP for the specific pattern you are implementing. LangGraph's Send API, astream_events, and streaming APIs change across releases — always verify current syntax from MCP before writing any workflow code.

pattern.name: sequential_pipeline
pattern.description: Fixed sequence of nodes. Each node receives the full state (all messages accumulated so far). Use for: multi-stage enrichment chains (fetch → validate → summarise).
pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Structural pattern (verify all method names and import paths via MCP): - StateGraph([state_type]) — use MessagesState unless stages need additional fields - add_node for each stage in the pipeline - add_edge(START, "stage_a") - add_edge("stage_a", "stage_b") - add_edge("stage_b", END) - compile(checkpointer=MemorySaver())
Call the compiled graph: result = await graph.ainvoke( input_state, config={"configurable": {"thread_id": session_id}} — verify structure via MCP )

pattern.name: parallel_fan_out
pattern.description: Multiple nodes run simultaneously on the same input using LangGraph's Send API. An aggregator node collects all results. Use for independent sub-queries that all contribute to the final answer.
pattern.structure: START → dispatcher → [node_a || node_b || node_c] → aggregator → END
pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
The Send API is the LangGraph-specific mechanism for parallel execution. MCP will provide the current import path for Send and the exact routing function signature.
Structural pattern (verify all types and method signatures via MCP): 1. State class needs an Annotated list field with an add reducer for collecting results from parallel branches — verify Annotated/reducer pattern via MCP 2. Dispatcher function: returns list of Send(node_name, state_slice) — one per item Verify: Send import path, constructor args via MCP 3. add_conditional_edges(START, dispatcher_fn, ["target_node"]) — verify signature via MCP 4. Target node executes in parallel for each Send 5. After parallel execution: aggregator node collects from the list field
For FIXED parallelism (known count, same operation): use asyncio.gather() inside a single node instead — much simpler when you don't need the Send API: results = await asyncio.gather(*[llm.ainvoke(msg) for msg in messages])

pattern.name: conditional_routing
pattern.description: Route to different nodes based on state values set by a classifier node. Use add_conditional_edges() for any branching logic. The supervisor pattern (2_orchestration_core.xml) is a specialisation of this.
pattern.structure: START → classifier → conditional_edge → [node_a | node_b | node_c] → END
pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Structural pattern (verify routing map format and method signatures via MCP): 1. State class: add a route_label field (set by classifier, read by routing function) 2. classifier_node: async function, calls LLM at temperature=0.0, extracts label returns {"route_label": label_string} 3. route_fn: pure function, reads route_label from state, returns target node name string Unknown labels: fall through to a default node (never crash on unexpected input) 4. add_conditional_edges("classifier", route_fn, routing_map_dict) routing_map_dict maps each returned label string to its target node name 5. Each routed node has an add_edge back to END
All method signatures and the exact routing map format must be verified via MCP.

pattern.name: streaming
pattern.description: Stream LLM tokens to the client as they are generated. Use graph.astream_events() for token-level streaming in a Server-Sent Events endpoint. Use graph.astream() for node-level streaming (complete node outputs).
pattern.implementation_guidance:
CRITICAL: The streaming API changes frequently — query MCP before implementing. CONDITIONAL: Run these queries ONLY if implementation.md requires a streaming endpoint. Phase 0 Step 4 does NOT run these — they are not in the baseline batch: search_docs_by_lang_chain("LangGraph astream_events streaming version v2 on_chat_model_stream current") search_docs_by_lang_chain("FastAPI StreamingResponse Server-Sent Events async generator current")
Key facts (verify every one of these with MCP before implementing): - Use graph.astream_events() not graph.astream() for token-level streaming - version="v2" (v1 is deprecated — always use v2, but confirm parameter name via MCP) - Filter events by event type string to extract token chunks (the event type string "on_chat_model_stream" may change — verify via MCP) - Chunk content is accessed via event data — verify exact access path via MCP - Wrap in FastAPI StreamingResponse with media_type="text/event-stream" - Send token chunks as SSE: f"data: {json.dumps({'token': chunk})}

" - Terminate stream with: "data: [DONE]

"
MCP will confirm: exact event type names, chunk content access pattern, and whether version="v2" is still the correct parameter for current LangGraph.
pattern.note: Always use version="v2" in astream_events — v1 is deprecated in recent LangGraph versions. Confirm via MCP.

pattern.name: long_running_async
pattern.description: For workflows that take more than 10 seconds, return a task_id immediately and let the client poll for the result. Use asyncio.create_task() to run the graph in the background.
pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Structural pattern (asyncio parts are stable — verify LangGraph invocation via MCP): 1. POST /tasks: generate task_id (uuid4), store {"status": "running"} in task store Launch asyncio.create_task(run()) to start the background workflow 2. run() coroutine: calls graph.ainvoke() with session config — verify config structure On success: update task store to {"status": "completed", "result": final_content} On error: update task store to {"status": "failed", "error": str(e)} 3. GET /tasks/{task_id}: return current status and result from task store
In-memory task store (dict) is acceptable for development. For production: use Redis or database-backed task tracking to survive restarts.

api_endpoints_needed.endpoint: POST /api/v1/tasks → returns task_id and initial status "running"
GET /api/v1/tasks/{task_id} → returns status, result (if complete), error (if failed)

guide.scenario: Requests need different specialists, chosen by an LLM at runtime
Data must be enriched step-by-step (fetch → validate → summarise)
Multiple independent sub-queries that can run simultaneously
guide.use: parallel_fan_out with Send API, or asyncio.gather() in a single node

guide.scenario: Different request types need different handlers (billing vs support)
User needs to see tokens stream from the LLM in real-time
guide.use: streaming with astream_events — MANDATORY MCP query for current API

best_practices.practice: All node functions are async — never call blocking I/O from inside a node.
Node functions return dict updates, not full state — LangGraph merges them via reducers.
Log the start and end of each graph invocation with the session_id (thread_id).
Set explicit timeouts on all Ollama calls — local LLM can take 120s+ on CPU hardware.
Graphs are composable — a sequential pipeline can call a subgraph at any node.
Never call graph.invoke() (sync) from inside a FastAPI async route — always use await graph.ainvoke().
Before implementing any LangGraph API call, run the relevant MCP query in this file — the API changes.
